reasoning: Confirmed. Line 78: $this->capability = apply_filters('regenerate_thumbs_cap', 'manage_options') with no minimum floor check. This value is used as the sole authorization gate at: (1) add_management_page() line 91 — determines who sees the admin page; (2) ajax_process_image() line 589 — capability check before proceeding to unlink() calls at lines 676-679 and 729-732; (3) ajax_finish_regen() line 556; (4) admin_enqueues() lines 117 and 131. The nonce 'force-regenerate-attachment' is NOT an independent barrier: it is generated server-side at line 192 (wp_localize_script) and embedded in the page that is rendered to any user who passes the capability check. If a co-installed plugin hooks 'regenerate_thumbs_cap' to return 'read', a subscriber can (a) access the admin page and obtain the nonce, then (b) POST to wp_ajax_regeneratethumbnail with a valid attachment ID and nonce, passing both the capability check and the nonce check, reaching unlink() on arbitrary thumbnail files in the uploads directory. The bulk_action_handler does not check capability at all — it only checks the bulk-media nonce and redirects to the admin page URL. The attack path requires a co-installed plugin lowering the capability; by default (manage_options) there is no vulnerability. The finding accurately characterizes a privilege-escalation-via-filter pattern with no floor, making it a real but conditional (medium severity) issue.
